question: In July 2, 1959 United States standards for grades of processed fruits, vegetables, and certain other products listed as dehydrated, consider the items in the "dried and dehydrated section" specifically marked as dehydrated along with any items in the Frozen/Chilled section that contain the whole name of the item, but not if they're marked Chilled. As of August 2023, what is the percentage (to the nearest percent) of those standards that have been superseded by a new version since the date given in the 1959 standards?

As of August 2023, what is the percentage (to the nearest percent) of those standards that have been superseded by a new version since the date given in the 1959 standards?

## Goal 1: Calculate the percentage of superseded dehydrated/frozen standards from the 1959 document as of August 2023

- Path 1.1: Direct USDA AMS archive and current CFR search
  - Approach: Search the USDA Agricultural Marketing Service (AMS) website for the publication "United States Standards for Grades of Processed Fruits, Vegetables, and Certain Other Products" dated July 2, 1959. Extract the list of items explicitly marked as dehydrated from the "dried and dehydrated section" and any items in the "Frozen/Chilled section" whose full name includes the item name (e.g., "dehydrated onions") but are not marked "Chilled". Then, for each identified standard, locate the current version on the USDA AMS website or in the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) as of August 2023. Determine if the current version has a later effective date than July 2, 1959 (indicating it has been superseded). Count superseded standards, compute the percentage rounded to the nearest whole percent.
  - Success: Successfully retrieved the 1959 document text, correctly identified all relevant items, accurately matched each to its latest version, and computed the percentage.

- Path 1.2: Federal Register and historical archive cross‑reference
  - Approach: Access the Federal Register for July 2, 1959, to find the original publication of the standards. Extract the dehydrated and frozen (non‑chilled) items as defined. Use the Wayback Machine or other historical snapshots to confirm the original list. Then search the current CFR (7 CFR parts 51 and 52) and the USDA AMS grade standards page for each item’s latest effective date. Confirm supersession if the current date is later than 1959. Count superseded items, calculate the percentage rounded to the nearest whole percent.
  - Success: Obtained the original list from legal archives, verified it against historical snapshots, and determined the supersession status for every item with high confidence; percentage computed correctly.

- Path 1.3: Combined web crawl and comparative analysis
  - Approach: Perform a targeted web search for the exact 1959 document title, then crawl the result pages to extract the relevant item list. Simultaneously, search for current USDA grade standards for processed fruits and vegetables, including any “dried and dehydrated” categories. For each item from the 1959 list, compare its current standard number or title to see if it has been replaced or updated. Use multiple trusted sources (USDA, FDA, CFR) to verify supersession dates. Tally superseded items, compute the percentage rounded to the nearest whole percent.
  - Success: Identified the 1959 document via web search, extracted the correct item set, and cross‑referenced all items with current sources to determine supersession; percentage accurately reported.

Identify and list all items in the "dried and dehydrated section" that are explicitly marked as dehydrated, plus any items in the "Frozen/Chilled" section whose full name includes the item name (e.g., "dehydrated onions") but exclude those marked "Chilled". 3. Calculate the percentage of superseded standards relative to the total number of relevant standards from the 1959 document, rounding to the nearest whole percent.

The original plan has a single goal with three execution paths. The goal is to calculate the percentage of superseded standards from the 1959 document – specifically items that are either:

- Listed in the "Frozen/Chilled section" and contain the whole name of a dehydrated item (i.e., the base commodity name), unless they are marked "Chilled".

The three planned paths were:

3. **Path 1.3**: Combined web crawl and comparative analysis.

All paths share the same overarching steps: (a) identify the 1959 list, (b) map each relevant standard to its latest version as of August 2023, (c) count superseded standards, (d) compute the percentage.

Frozen Apples still has effective date May 17, 1954 with only corrected reprints (not superseded as of Aug 2023). Frozen Grapefruit Juice, Concentrated had a 3rd issue effective Oct 1, 1970 (supersedes Dec 28, 1956 original). Frozen Orange Juice, Concentrated had a 4th issue effective Sept 21, 1968 or Oct 19, 1964 (supersedes Dec 1, 1955 original). Therefore 5 out of 6 standards have been superseded, giving 83.33% rounded to 83%.

In July 2, 1959 United States standards for grades of processed fruits, vegetables, and certain other products listed as dehydrated, consider the items in the "dried and dehydrated section" specifically marked as dehydrated along with any items in the Frozen/Chilled section that contain the whole name of the item, but not if they're marked Chilled. As of August 2023, what is the percentage (to the nearest percent) of those standards that have been superseded by a new version since the date given in the 1959 standards?

————Memory System Guidance————
## Planning Guidance
1. Locate the official USDA publication "United States Standards for Grades of Processed Fruits, Vegetables, and Certain Other Products" from July 2, 1959 (e.g., via the USDA Agricultural Marketing Service archives or the Federal Register). Identify and list all items in the "dried and dehydrated section" that are explicitly marked as dehydrated, plus any items in the "Frozen/Chilled" section whose full name includes the item name (e.g., "dehydrated onions") but exclude those marked "Chilled". This defines the set of standards to track.

2. For each standard in that set, search the current USDA AMS website or the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) to determine the latest version effective as of August 2023. Note which standards have been superseded (i.e., replaced by a newer version issued after July 2, 1959) and which remain unchanged.

3. Calculate the percentage of superseded standards relative to the total number of relevant standards from the 1959 document, rounding to the nearest whole percent.

## Experience Guidance
To determine the percentage of superseded standards, first locate the original July 2, 1959 document and identify all items in the "dried and dehydrated section" marked as dehydrated, plus any items in the Frozen/Chilled section that contain the whole item name but are not marked Chilled. Then, compare each standard against the latest version as of August 2023, using official government sources (e.g., USDA or FDA archives) and cross-referencing with historical snapshots (e.g., Wayback Machine) to verify supersession dates. Finally, count the superseded standards, compute the percentage rounded to the nearest whole number, and double-check your categorization to avoid including irrelevant items.
————End Memory————

- Path 1.1: Direct USDA AMS archive and current CFR search

Extract the list of items explicitly marked as dehydrated from the "dried and dehydrated section" and any items in the "Frozen/Chilled section" whose full name includes the item name (e.g., "dehydrated onions") but are not marked "Chilled". Then, for each identified standard, locate the current version on the USDA AMS website or in the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) as of August 2023. Determine if the current version has a later effective date than July 2, 1959 (indicating it has been superseded). Count superseded standards, compute the percentage rounded to the nearest whole percent.

- Path 1.2: Federal Register and historical archive cross‑reference

Use the Wayback Machine or other historical snapshots to confirm the original list. Then search the current CFR (7 CFR parts 51 and 52) and the USDA AMS grade standards page for each item’s latest effective date. Confirm supersession if the current date is later than 1959. Count superseded items, calculate the percentage rounded to the nearest whole percent.
  - Success: Obtained the original list from legal archives, verified it against historical snapshots, and determined the supersession status for every item with high confidence; percentage computed correctly.

- Path 1.3: Combined web crawl and comparative analysis
  - Approach: Perform a targeted web search for the exact 1959 document title, then crawl the result pages to extract the relevant item list. Simultaneously, search for current USDA grade standards for processed fruits and vegetables, including any “dried and dehydrated” categories. For each item from the 1959 list, compare its current standard number or title to see if it has been replaced or updated. Use multiple trusted sources (USDA, FDA, CFR) to verify supersession dates. Tally superseded items, compute the percentage rounded to the nearest whole percent.
